sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, describing a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is painful and occasionally requires him to wash down solid foods.
He has never fully choked but has had occasional episodes of food getting stuck, which he attributes to solids rather than liquids.
He denies coughing, blood in stool or vomit, weight loss, chest pain, or shortness of breath.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He has been taking metformin 500mg twice daily and norvasc.
His hemoglobin A1c was previously 7.4.
Review of systems: The patient denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or other side effects from metformin.
He reports that his blood sugars are generally within target range and has been adhering to his medication regimen.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice daily for diabetes
Norvasc for high blood pressure
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure in the office is 120/50.

Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine with no swelling, thyroid feels normal, no masses or lymph nodes are palpable, throat examination is unremarkable, lung exam is clear, heart exam is normal, no murmurs are present, belly exam reveals epigastric tenderness but no masses or significant swelling, back exam is normal, and extremity and neurological exams are fine.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 56-year-old male presenting with difficulty swallowing, suspected narrowing of the esophagus causing dysphagia.
Problem list: Dysphagia; Hypertension; Diabetes Mellitus; Esophageal Stricture; Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease.
Plan:
I prescribed Prilosec 20mg once daily for gastroesophageal reflux disease.
I recommended that the patient chew their foods frequently, especially hard or sticky foods, and have a liquid to help facilitate swallowing.
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to investigate the cause of the dysphagia.
I will also order another hemoglobin A1c and kidney function tests.
I will refer the patient to ophthalmology for an eye examination to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and norvasc as previously prescribed.
I will have the patient return in one month for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month for follow-up to review the results of the blood tests and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
I will also check on the patient's progress with the Prilosec and refer him to the emergency department if he experiences any worsening symptoms.
